input.topic: Advanced Control Systems and Signal Processing
input.message: Create a comprehensive course outline for a graduate-level course that bridges classical control theory with modern digital signal processing. Cover Laplace and Z-transforms, state-space representations, PID tuning, frequency-domain analysis (Bode, Nyquist), discrete-time systems, FIR/IIR filter design, adaptive filtering, and an introduction to optimal control (LQR/LQG). The course should include MATLAB/Simulink lab sessions and a capstone project integrating both domains. Emphasize real-world applications in robotics and autonomous systems.

learning_objectives: Identify the major organelles of eukaryotic cells and describe their functions
Compare and contrast prokaryotic and eukaryotic cells
activities_projects: Microscope lab: observing plant and animal cells
input.message: Plan a single 90-minute lecture-lab session for first-year biology students.

input.class_title: Quantitative Data Analysis and Statistical Inference
learning_objectives: Apply descriptive statistics to summarize survey data
Formulate and test null and alternative hypotheses using t-tests and chi-square tests
Interpret p-values and confidence intervals in the context of social science research
Evaluate the assumptions underlying parametric statistical tests
Use SPSS or R to perform basic inferential analysis on a provided dataset
key_topics: Measures of central tendency and dispersion
activities_projects: Guided SPSS walkthrough: importing a CSV dataset and computing descriptive statistics
Small-group exercise: formulating hypotheses for a mock study and choosing the appropriate test
Hands-on analysis: running a t-test on provided survey data and writing an APA-style results paragraph
Peer review: swapping results sections with a partner and providing structured feedback
input.message: Design a detailed 3-hour seminar session for second-year social science undergraduates who have completed an introductory statistics module. Include transitions between lecture segments and activities.

input.learning_objective: Explain the core ideas of the Enlightenment and evaluate their influence on modern democratic thought
key_points: Key Enlightenment thinkers: Voltaire, Rousseau, Locke, Montesquieu
Central concepts: reason, natural rights, separation of powers
Impact on the French and American revolutions
input.message: Create a focused lecture presentation for a 50-minute undergraduate history class.

input.learning_objective: Analyze conduction, convection, and radiation heat transfer modes and apply the first and second laws of thermodynamics to engineering systems
key_points: First law of thermodynamics: energy conservation in closed and open systems
Second law: entropy, irreversibility, and Carnot efficiency
Fourier's law of heat conduction and thermal resistance networks
Convective heat transfer coefficients and Newton's law of cooling
Radiative heat transfer: Stefan-Boltzmann law and view factors
Combined heat transfer in real-world heat exchangers
input.lesson_breakdown: Begin with a 15-minute review of thermodynamic state variables. Proceed to a 30-minute derivation-heavy lecture on the first and second laws with worked examples. Follow with a 20-minute segment on conduction and convection fundamentals. Conclude with a 25-minute case study of a shell-and-tube heat exchanger design.
input.activities: Solve two worked problems: (1) calculate heat loss through a composite wall, (2) size a counterflow heat exchanger given inlet/outlet temperatures and flow rates.
input.homework: Problem set: 5 problems covering combined conduction-convection analysis and a short Carnot cycle efficiency calculation.
input.extra_activities: Optional simulation exercise: use an online heat exchanger sizing tool to explore the effect of changing flow arrangements (parallel vs. counter-flow) on effectiveness.
input.message: Create a comprehensive presentation for a 90-minute graduate engineering lecture. Include detailed derivation slides, worked examples, and diagrams.

key_topics: Primitive data types (int, float, string, boolean)

key_topics: Le Chatelier's principle and equilibrium constant expressions
pH calculations for strong and weak acids/bases
Standard reduction potentials and Nernst equation
input.additional_instructions: Include at least one multi-step calculation problem in the short answer section. Essay questions should require students to compare and contrast related concepts (e.g., galvanic vs. electrolytic cells). Create a comprehensive midterm exam covering the second half of a university General Chemistry II course. Target duration: 90 minutes.
